      },
      body: JSON.stringify({
        model: 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001',
        max_tokens: 500,
        messages: [{
          role: 'user',
          content: `You are a casting director assistant. Today's date is ${todayStr}. Extract casting criteria from this natural language query and return ONLY valid JSON with no markdown:

Query: "${query}"

Return this exact JSON structure:
{
  "gender": "male" or "female" or "any",
  "age_min": null or number,
  "age_max": null or number,
  "hair_color": null or string,
  "eye_color": null or string,
  "body_type": null or string,
  "ethnicity": null or string,
  "height_min_cm": null or number,
  "height_max_cm": null or number,
  "special_skills": [],
  "union_only": false or true,
  "availability_date": null or "YYYY-MM-DD" (resolve relative dates like "Tuesday", "next Thursday", "June 20" against today ${todayStr}),
  "location_text": null or string (any city or place name mentioned, e.g. "Vancouver", "Okanagan"),
  "interpretation": "one sentence describing what was understood"
}`,
        }],
      }),
    })

    const data = await res.json()
    const text: string = data.content?.[0]?.text || '{}'
    const criteria = JSON.parse(text.replace(/```json\n?/g, '').replace(/```\n?/g, '').trim())

    // Resolve location_text to a region code if no shootRegionCode was passed in
    let resolvedRegionCode: string | null = null
    let effectiveRegion = shootRegionCode || ''
    if (criteria.location_text && !shootRegionCode) {
      const fromCity = getRegionFromCity(criteria.location_text)
      if (fromCity) {
        resolvedRegionCode = fromCity
        effectiveRegion = fromCity
      }
    }

    // Filter performers by extracted criteria
    let filtered = allPerformers.filter(p => {
      if (criteria.gender && criteria.gender !== 'any') {
        if (p.gender?.toLowerCase() !== criteria.gender.toLowerCase()) return false
      }
      if (criteria.age_min && p.age && p.age < criteria.age_min) return false
      if (criteria.age_max && p.age && p.age > criteria.age_max) return false
      if (criteria.hair_color && p.hair_color) {
        if (!p.hair_color.toLowerCase().includes(criteria.hair_color.toLowerCase())) return false
      }
      if (criteria.union_only && (p.union_priority ?? 4) > 2) return false
      if (criteria.height_min_cm && p.height_cm && p.height_cm < criteria.height_min_cm) return false
      if (criteria.height_max_cm && p.height_cm && p.height_cm > criteria.height_max_cm) return false
      return true
    })

    // Location filter
    if (effectiveRegion) {
      filtered = filtered.filter(p => {
        if (!p.film_region_code) return true
        return getMatchingRegions(effectiveRegion, p.film_region_code, p.travel_willingness || 'local', p.travel_radius_km || 100)
      })
    }

    // Sort by union priority ASC (Full Members first)
    filtered.sort((a, b) => (a.union_priority ?? 4) - (b.union_priority ?? 4))

    return {
      performers: filtered,
      interpretation: criteria.interpretation || `Search for: ${query}`,
      availability_date: criteria.availability_date || null,
      resolved_region_code: resolvedRegionCode,
    }
  } catch {
    return {
      performers: allPerformers.slice(0, 20),
      interpretation: `Could not parse: "${query}" â€” showing all performers`,
      availability_date: null,
      resolved_region_code: null,
    }
  }
}

===== C:\Users\One\Desktop\setready\lib\booking-weather.ts =====
import { resolveLocationToRegion } from './film-regions'

// â”€â”€ Location resolution â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

export function resolveLocationToCoords(
  locationText: string | null | undefined
): { lat: number; lng: number; regionName: string } | null {
  if (!locationText?.trim()) return null
  const r = resolveLocationToRegion(locationText.trim())
  if (!r) return null
  return { lat: r.lat, lng: r.lng, regionName: r.name }
}

// â”€â”€ Weather advisory â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

export function computeAdvisory(precipProb: number, tempMax: number, windspeed: number): string {
  if (precipProb >= 60) return 'Rain likely â€” bring rain gear'
  if (precipProb >= 30) return 'Possible showers â€” pack a layer'
  if (tempMax <= 5) return 'Cold â€” dress warm under wardrobe'
  if (windspeed >= 50) return 'Windy on set'
  return 'Clear conditions expected'
}

export function advisoryIcon(advisory: string): string {
  if (advisory.startsWith('Rain likely')) return 'ðŸŒ§ï¸'
  if (advisory.startsWith('Possible')) return 'ðŸŒ¦ï¸'
  if (advisory.startsWith('Cold')) return 'ðŸ¥¶'
  if (advisory.startsWith('Windy')) return 'ðŸ’¨'
  return 'â˜€ï¸'
}

// â”€â”€ Weather result type â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

export interface WeatherResult {
  tempMax: number
  tempMin: number
  precipProb: number
  windspeed: number
  advisory: string
  icon: string
  regionName: string
}

// â”€â”€ Open-Meteo fetch (16-day window, no key required) â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

const FORECAST_WINDOW_DAYS = 16

export async function fetchBookingWeather(
  locationText: string | null | undefined,
  shootDate: string
): Promise<WeatherResult | null> {
  const coords = resolveLocationToCoords(locationText)
  if (!coords) return null

  const today = new Date()
  today.setHours(0, 0, 0, 0)
  const shoot = new Date(shootDate + 'T00:00:00')
  const daysAway = Math.ceil((shoot.getTime() - today.getTime()) / 86400000)
  if (daysAway > FORECAST_WINDOW_DAYS || daysAway < 0) return null

  const url =
    `https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast` +
    `?latitude=${coords.lat}&longitude=${coords.lng}` +
    `&daily=temperature_2m_max,temperature_2m_min,precipitation_probability_max,weathercode,windspeed_10m_max` +
    `&timezone=America%2FVancouver` +
    `&start_date=${shootDate}&end_date=${shootDate}`

  try {
    const res = await fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: 3600 } } as RequestInit)
    if (!res.ok) return null

    const data = await res.json()
    const daily = data.daily

    const tempMax = daily?.temperature_2m_max?.[0]
    if (tempMax === undefined || tempMax === null) return null

    const tempMin = Math.round(daily.temperature_2m_min[0] ?? 0)
    const precipProb = daily.precipitation_probability_max[0] ?? 0
    const windspeed = Math.round(daily.windspeed_10m_max[0] ?? 0)
    const advisory = computeAdvisory(precipProb, Math.round(tempMax), windspeed)

    return {
      tempMax: Math.round(tempMax),
      tempMin,
      precipProb,
      windspeed,
      advisory,
      icon: advisoryIcon(advisory),
      regionName: coords.regionName,
    }
  } catch {
    return null
  }
}

===== C:\Users\One\Desktop\setready\lib\casting-auth.ts =====
import { SignJWT, jwtVerify } from 'jose'
import { cookies } from 'next/headers'
import { createClient } from '@supabase/supabase-js'

const supabaseAdmin = createClient(
  process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
  process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY!
)

const [[REDACTED]]
  process.env.CASTING_JWT_SECRET ||
  'setready-casting-secret-2026-change-in-prod'
)

export async function createSession(
  accountId: string,
  accountType: 'agent' | 'casting_director',
  email: string,
  name: string
) {
  const token = await new SignJWT({
    accountId, accountType, email, name
  })
    .setProtectedHeader({ alg: 'HS256' })
    .setExpirationTime('7d')
    .sign(JWT_SECRET)

  await supabaseAdmin.from('casting_sessions').insert({
    account_type: accountType,
    account_id: accountId,
    token,
    expires_at: new Date(
      Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000
    ).toISOString()
  })

  return token
}

export async function verifySession(
  token: string
): Promise<{
  accountId: string
  accountType: string
  email: string
  name: string
} | null> {
  try {
    const { payload } = await jwtVerify(token, JWT_SECRET)
    return payload as any
  } catch {
    return null
  }
}

export async function getAgentSession() {
  const cookieStore = await cookies()
  const token = cookieStore.get('agent_session')?.value
  if (!token) return null
  return verifySession(token)
}

export async function getCastingSession() {
  const cookieStore = await cookies()
  const token = cookieStore.get('casting_session')?.value
  if (!token) return null
  return verifySession(token)
}

export async function deleteSession(token: string) {
  await supabaseAdmin
    .from('casting_sessions')
    .delete()
    .eq('token', token)
}

export { supabaseAdmin }

===== C:\Users\One\Desktop\setready\lib\casting-notify.ts =====
import { createClient } from '@supabase/supabase-js'
import {
  sendEmail,
  castingRequestEmailHtml,
  submissionConfirmedEmailHtml,
  agentConfirmationEmailHtml,
} from '@/lib/email'

const supabaseAdmin = createClient(
  process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
  process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY!
)

async function writeMessages(records: any[]) {
  if (!records.length) return
  await supabaseAdmin.from('messages').insert(records)
}

// â”€â”€ Single-recipient in-app notification â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

export async function notify({
  recipientType,
  recipientId,
  type,
  title,
  message,
  actionUrl,
  relatedRequestId,
  relatedSubmissionId,
}: {
  recipientType: 'performer' | 'agent' | 'casting_director' | 'admin'
  recipientId: string
  type: string
  title: string
  message: string
  actionUrl?: string
  relatedRequestId?: string
  relatedSubmissionId?: string
}) {
  await supabaseAdmin.from('casting_notifications').insert({
    recipient_type: recipientType,
    recipient_id: recipientId,
    type,
    title,
    message,
    action_url: actionUrl,
    related_request_id: relatedRequestId,
    related_submission_id: relatedSubmissionId,
  })
}

// â”€â”€ Notify all agents (in-app + optional email) â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

export async function notifyAllAgents(
  type: string,
  title: string,
  message: string,
  actionUrl?: string,
  relatedRequestId?: string,
  castingRequestData?: any,
  sendEmailNotification = false
) {
  const { data: agents } = await supabaseAdmin
    .from('agent_accounts')
    .select('id, email, name, agency_id, agencies(name)')
    .eq('is_active', true)

  if (!agents?.length) return

  const notifications = agents.map(agent => ({
    recipient_type: 'agent' as const,
    recipient_id: agent.id,
    type,
    title,
    message,
    action_url: actionUrl,
    related_request_id: relatedRequestId,
  }))

  await supabaseAdmin.from('casting_notifications').insert(notifications)

  // Mirror to messages table
  await writeMessages(
    agents.map(agent => ({
      sender_type: 'system',
      sender_name: 'SetReady Casting',
      recipient_type: 'agent',
      recipient_id: agent.id,
      subject: title,
      body: message,
      message_type: 'casting_request',
      action_url: actionUrl,
      action_label: 'View Request',
      related_id: relatedRequestId || null,
    }))
  )

  if (sendEmailNotification && castingRequestData) {
    for (const agent of agents) {
      if (!agent.email) continue
      await sendEmail({
        to: agent.email,
        subject: `New Casting Request: ${castingRequestData.production_name}`,
        html: castingRequestEmailHtml({
          productionName: castingRequestData.production_name,
          shootDate: castingRequestData.shoot_date,
          location: castingRequestData.location,
          roleType: castingRequestData.role_type,
          sceneDescription: castingRequestData.description,
          rate: castingRequestData.rate,
          numberNeeded: castingRequestData.performers_needed,
          recipientName: agent.name || 'Agent',
          recipientType: 'agent',
        }),
      })
    }
  }
}

// â”€â”€ Notify independent performers (in-app + optional email) â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

export async function notifyIndependentPerformers(
  castingRequestData: any,
  sendEmailToPerformers: boolean
) {
  const { data: setting } = await supabaseAdmin
    .from('admin_settings')
    .select('value')
    .eq('key', 'notify_independent_performers')
    .maybeSingle()

  if (setting?.value !== 'true') return

  const [{ data: rosterData }, { data: exclusionData }] = await Promise.all([
    supabaseAdmin.from('agency_roster').select('user_id').eq('status', 'active'),
    supabaseAdmin.from('casting_notification_exclusions').select('user_id'),
  ])

  const agencyIds = new Set((rosterData || []).map((r: any) => r.user_id))
  const excludedIds = new Set((exclusionData || []).map((e: any) => e.user_id))

  const { data: performers } = await supabaseAdmin
    .from('performer_profiles')
    .select('id')
    .eq('is_public', true)

  const independentIds = (performers || [])
    .map((p: any) => p.id)
    .filter((id: string) => !agencyIds.has(id) && !excludedIds.has(id))

  if (!independentIds.length) return

  // In-app notifications
  const notifications = independentIds.map((userId: string) => ({
    recipient_type: 'performer' as const,
    recipient_id: userId,
    type: 'new_casting_request',
    title: `New Casting: ${castingRequestData.production_name}`,
    message: `A casting request has been posted for ${castingRequestData.shoot_date}. As a self-represented performer, you may be contacted directly.`,
    action_url: '/casting-portal',
    related_request_id: castingRequestData.id,
  }))

  await supabaseAdmin.from('casting_notifications').insert(notifications)

  // Mirror to messages table
  await writeMessages(
    independentIds.map((userId: string) => ({
      sender_type: 'system',
      sender_name: 'SetReady Casting',
      recipient_type: 'performer',
      recipient_id: userId,
      subject: `New Casting: ${castingRequestData.production_name}`,
      body: `A casting request has been posted for ${castingRequestData.shoot_date}. As a self-represented performer, you may be contacted directly.`,
      message_type: 'casting_request',
      action_url: '/casting-portal',
      action_label: 'View Request',
      related_id: castingRequestData.id,
    }))
  )

  // Emails (only if enabled)
  if (!sendEmailToPerformers) return

  // Fetch user emails in one query
  const { data: users } = await supabaseAdmin
    .from('users')
    .select('id, email, name')
    .in('id', independentIds)

  for (const user of users || []) {
    if (!user.email) continue
    await sendEmail({
      to: user.email,
      subject: `New Casting Request: ${castingRequestData.production_name}`,
      html: castingRequestEmailHtml({
        productionName: castingRequestData.production_name,
        shootDate: castingRequestData.shoot_date,
        location: castingRequestData.location,
        roleType: castingRequestData.role_type,
        sceneDescription: castingRequestData.description,
        rate: castingRequestData.rate,
        numberNeeded: castingRequestData.performers_needed,
        recipientName: user.name || 'Performer',
        recipientType: 'performer',
      }),
    })
  }
}

// â”€â”€ Notify agent when their performer is confirmed â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

export async function notifyAgentOfConfirmation(
  agentId: string,
  performerName: string,
  castingRequestData: any
) {
  const { data: agent } = await supabaseAdmin
    .from('agent_accounts')
    .select('email, name')
    .eq('id', agentId)
    .single()

  if (!agent) return

  await supabaseAdmin.from('casting_notifications').insert({
    recipient_type: 'agent',
    recipient_id: agentId,
    type: 'performer_confirmed',
    title: `${performerName} Confirmed`,
    message: `${performerName} has been confirmed for ${castingRequestData.production_name}`,
    action_url: '/agent/dashboard',
    related_request_id: castingRequestData.id,
  })

  // Mirror to messages table
  await writeMessages([{
    sender_type: 'system',
    sender_name: 'SetReady Casting',
    recipient_type: 'agent',
    recipient_id: agentId,
    subject: `${performerName} Confirmed`,
    body: `${performerName} has been confirmed for ${castingRequestData.production_name}.`,
    message_type: 'booking_confirmed',
    action_url: '/agent/dashboard',
    action_label: 'View Dashboard',
    related_id: castingRequestData.id,
  }])

  if (agent.email) {
    await sendEmail({
      to: agent.email,
      subject: `âœ… ${performerName} confirmed for ${castingRequestData.production_name}`,
      html: agentConfirmationEmailHtml({
        agentName: agent.name || 'Agent',
        performerName,
        productionName: castingRequestData.production_name,
        shootDate: castingRequestData.shoot_date,
      }),
    })
  }
}

// â”€â”€ Notify performer when confirmed â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

export async function notifyPerformerOfConfirmation(
  performerUserId: string,
  castingRequestData: any,
  agencyName: string
) {
  const { data: user } = await supabaseAdmin
    .from('users')
    .select('email, name')
    .eq('id', performerUserId)
    .single()

  if (!user) return

  await supabaseAdmin.from('casting_notifications').insert({
    recipient_type: 'performer',
    recipient_id: performerUserId,
    type: 'booking_confirmed',
    title: "ðŸŽ‰ You've been confirmed!",
    message: `You have been confirmed for ${castingRequestData.production_name} on ${castingRequestData.shoot_date}. Your agency will be in touch.`,
    action_url: '/dashboard',
    related_request_id: castingRequestData.id,
  })

  // Mirror to messages table
  await writeMessages([{
    sender_type: 'system',
    sender_name: 'SetReady Casting',
    recipient_type: 'performer',
    recipient_id: performerUserId,
    subject: `ðŸŽ‰ You've been confirmed for ${castingRequestData.production_name}!`,
    body: `Congratulations! You have been confirmed for ${castingRequestData.production_name} on ${castingRequestData.shoot_date}. Your agency will be in touch with further details.`,
    message_type: 'booking_confirmed',
    action_url: '/dashboard',
    action_label: 'View Dashboard',
    related_id: castingRequestData.id,
  }])

  if (user.email) {
    await sendEmail({
      to: user.email,
      subject: `ðŸŽ‰ You're confirmed for ${castingRequestData.production_name}!`,
      html: submissionConfirmedEmailHtml({
        performerName: user.name || 'Performer',
        productionName: castingRequestData.production_name,
        shootDate: castingRequestData.shoot_date,
        location: castingRequestData.location,
        callTime: castingRequestData.call_time,
        agencyName,
      }),
    })
  }
}

interface CertificateData {
  recipientName: string;
  courseName: string;
  score: number;
  issueDate: string;
  certificateId: string;
}

export async function generateCertificatePDF(data: CertificateData): Promise<Buffer> {
  const verificationUrl = `${process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL || 'http://localhost:3000'}/verify/${data.certificateId}`;
  const qrCodeDataURL = await QRCode.toDataURL(verificationUrl, {
    width: 120,
    margin: 1,
    color: { dark: '#1a1a2e', light: '#ffffff' },
  });

  const CertificatePDF = () => (
    <Document>
      <Page size="A4" style={styles.page}>
        <Text style={styles.watermark}>SET READY</Text>
        <View style={styles.border}>
          <View style={styles.innerBorder}>
            <View style={styles.header}>
              <Text style={styles.logo}>SET READY</Text>
              <Text style={styles.logoSubtitle}>FILM INDUSTRY TRAINING</Text>
              <View style={styles.decorativeLine} />
            </View>
            
            <Text style={styles.title}>Certificate of Completion</Text>
            <Text style={styles.subtitle}>This certificate is proudly presented to</Text>
            <Text style={styles.recipientName}>{data.recipientName}</Text>
            <Text style={styles.achievementText}>for successfully completing the course</Text>
            <Text style={styles.courseName}>{data.courseName}</Text>
            
            <View style={styles.scoreBox}>
              <Text style={styles.scoreLabel}>Final Score</Text>
              <Text style={styles.scoreValue}>{data.score}%</Text>
            </View>
            
            <View style={styles.dateSection}>
              <Text style={styles.dateLabel}>Date of Achievement</Text>
              <Text style={styles.dateValue}>{data.issueDate}</Text>
            </View>
            
            <View style={styles.signatureSection}>
              <Text style={styles.signatureName}>M.B</Text>
              <View style={styles.signatureLine} />
              <Text style={styles.signatureTitle}>Program Director</Text>
            </View>
            
            <View style={styles.goldSticker}>
              <View style={styles.stickerCircle}>
                <View style={styles.stickerInner}>
                  <Text style={styles.stickerStar}>â˜…</Text>
                  <Text style={styles.stickerText}>VERIFIED</Text>
                  <Text style={styles.stickerText}>AUTHENTIC</Text>
                </View>
              </View>
            </View>
            
            <View style={styles.footer}>
              <Text style={styles.certificateId}>ID: {data.certificateId}</Text>
              <Image src={qrCodeDataURL} style={styles.qrCode} />
            </View>
          </View>
        </View>
      </Page>
    </Document>
  );

  const { renderToBuffer } = await import('@react-pdf/renderer');
  const buffer = await renderToBuffer(<CertificatePDF />);
  return Buffer.from(buffer);
}

===== C:\Users\One\Desktop\setready\lib\compress-image.ts =====
const MAX_EDGE = 1200
const QUALITY = 0.82

export function compressImage(file: File): Promise<File> {
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const objectUrl = URL.createObjectURL(file)
    const img = new window.Image()

    img.onerror = () => {
      URL.revokeObjectURL(objectUrl)
      reject(new Error('Failed to load image for compression'))
    }

    img.onload = () => {
      URL.revokeObjectURL(objectUrl)

      let { width, height } = img
      if (Math.max(width, height) > MAX_EDGE) {
        if (width >= height) {
          height = Math.round((height * MAX_EDGE) / width)
          width = MAX_EDGE
        } else {
          width = Math.round((width * MAX_EDGE) / height)
          height = MAX_EDGE
        }
      }

      const canvas = document.createElement('canvas')
      canvas.width = width
      canvas.height = height
      const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d')
      if (!ctx) { reject(new Error('Canvas 2D context unavailable')); return }
      ctx.drawImage(img, 0, 0, width, height)

      const baseName = file.name.replace(/\.[^.]+$/, '')

      canvas.toBlob(
        (webpBlob) => {
          if (webpBlob) {
            resolve(new File([webpBlob], `${baseName}.webp`, { type: 'image/webp' }))
            return
          }
          // Fallback: WebP unsupported â€” try JPEG
          canvas.toBlob(
            (jpegBlob) => {
              if (jpegBlob) {
                resolve(new File([jpegBlob], `${baseName}.jpg`, { type: 'image/jpeg' }))
              } else {
                reject(new Error('Image compression produced no output'))
              }
            },
            'image/jpeg',
            QUALITY
          )
        },
        'image/webp',
        QUALITY
      )
    }

    img.src = objectUrl
  })
}

===== C:\Users\One\Desktop\setready\lib\email.ts =====
import { Resend } from 'resend'

const resend = new Resend(process.env.RESEND_API_KEY)

const FROM_EMAIL = 'SetReady <notifications@setready.site>'

function isEmailEnabled() {
  return !!process.env.RESEND_API_KEY
}

export async function sendEmail({
  to,
  subject,
  html,
  text,
}: {
  to: string | string[]
  subject: string
  html: string
  text?: string
}) {
  if (!isEmailEnabled()) {
    console.log('[Email] Not configured. Would have sent to:', to, '| Subject:', subject)
    return { success: false, reason: 'not configured' }
  }

  try {
    const result = await resend.emails.send({
      from: FROM_EMAIL,
      to: Array.isArray(to) ? to : [to],
      subject,
      html,
      text: text || subject,
    })
    return { success: true, data: result }
  } catch (err) {
    console.error('[Email] Send error:', err)
    return { success: false, error: err }
  }
}

export interface FilmRegion {
  code: string
  name: string
  provinceCode: string
  cities: string[]
  adjacentRegions: string[]
  latitudeCenter: number
  longitudeCenter: number
}

// Haversine distance between two lat/lng points in km
export function calculateDistanceKm(
  lat1: number, lng1: number,
  lat2: number, lng2: number
): number {
  const R = 6371
  const dLat = (lat2 - lat1) * Math.PI / 180
  const dLng = (lng2 - lng1) * Math.PI / 180
  const a =
    Math.sin(dLat / 2) * Math.sin(dLat / 2) +
    Math.cos(lat1 * Math.PI / 180) * Math.cos(lat2 * Math.PI / 180) *
    Math.sin(dLng / 2) * Math.sin(dLng / 2)
  return R * 2 * Math.atan2(Math.sqrt(a), Math.sqrt(1 - a))
}

// All region codes within radiusKm of sourceRegionCode
export function getRegionsWithinRadius(sourceRegionCode: string, radiusKm: number): string[] {
  const source = FILM_REGIONS[sourceRegionCode]
  if (!source) return [sourceRegionCode]

  return Object.values(FILM_REGIONS)
    .filter(r => calculateDistanceKm(
      source.latitudeCenter, source.longitudeCenter,
      r.latitudeCenter, r.longitudeCenter
    ) <= radiusKm)
    .map(r => r.code)
}

// Does performer's region match the shoot region given their travel settings?
export function getMatchingRegions(
  shootRegionCode: string,
  performerRegionCode: string,
  travelWillingness: string,
  travelRadiusKm: number
): boolean {
  if (shootRegionCode === performerRegionCode) return true

  const shootRegion = FILM_REGIONS[shootRegionCode]
  const performerRegion = FILM_REGIONS[performerRegionCode]
  if (!shootRegion || !performerRegion) return false

  if (travelWillingness === 'national') return true

  if (travelWillingness === 'provincial') {
    return shootRegion.provinceCode === performerRegion.provinceCode
  }

  // local or regional â€” distance-based
  const distance = calculateDistanceKm(
    shootRegion.latitudeCenter, shootRegion.longitudeCenter,
    performerRegion.latitudeCenter, performerRegion.longitudeCenter
  )
  return distance <= travelRadiusKm
}

export function getProvinceRegions(provinceCode: string): string[] {
  return Object.values(FILM_REGIONS)
    .filter(r => r.provinceCode === provinceCode)
    .map(r => r.code)
}

export function getRegionName(code: string): string {
  return FILM_REGIONS[code]?.name || code
}

// â”€â”€ Internal normalization helpers â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

const PROVINCE_WORDS = [
  'british columbia', 'alberta', 'ontario', 'quÃ©bec', 'quebec', 'saskatchewan',
  'manitoba', 'nova scotia', 'new brunswick', 'newfoundland and labrador',
  'newfoundland', 'prince edward island', 'northwest territories',
  'nunavut', 'yukon', 'canada',
  'bc', 'ab', 'on', 'qc', 'sk', 'mb', 'ns', 'nb', 'nl', 'pe', 'pei', 'yt', 'nt', 'nu',
]

const QUALIFIER_PREFIXES = [
  'downtown', 'uptown', 'midtown', 'greater', 'metro', 'central',
  'north', 'south', 'east', 'west', 'northeast', 'northwest', 'southeast', 'southwest',
]

function normalizeLocationInput(input: string): string {
  let s = input.toLowerCase().trim()

  // Strip everything after the first comma: "Vancouver, BC" â†’ "vancouver"
  const commaIdx = s.indexOf(',')
  if (commaIdx !== -1) s = s.slice(0, commaIdx).trim()

  // Strip province words at the tail or head (whole-word boundary)
  for (const prov of PROVINCE_WORDS) {
    if (s === prov) return ''
    if (s.endsWith(' ' + prov)) s = s.slice(0, s.length - prov.length - 1).trim()
    if (s.startsWith(prov + ' ')) s = s.slice(prov.length + 1).trim()
  }

  // Strip leading qualifier words â€” loop until stable (handles "Greater North Vancouver")
  let changed = true
  while (changed) {
    changed = false
    for (const q of QUALIFIER_PREFIXES) {
      if (s.startsWith(q + ' ')) {
        s = s.slice(q.length + 1).trim()
        changed = true
        break
      }
    }
  }

  return s
}

// â”€â”€ Region lookup â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

export function getRegionFromCity(city: string): string | null {
  if (!city?.trim()) return null

  const normalized = normalizeLocationInput(city)
  if (!normalized) return null

  const regions = Object.values(FILM_REGIONS)

  // Pass 1: exact match (normalized input vs lowercase city name)
  for (const region of regions) {
    if (region.cities.some(c => c.toLowerCase() === normalized)) {
      return region.code
    }
  }

  // Pass 2: contains in both directions (min 4 chars each side to avoid false hits)
  if (normalized.length >= 4) {
    for (const region of regions) {
      if (region.cities.some(c => {
        const cn = c.toLowerCase()
        return cn.length >= 4 && (normalized.includes(cn) || cn.includes(normalized))
      })) {
        return region.code
      }
    }
  }

  return null
}

// Returns region metadata alongside the code â€” used by the weather/commute layer
export function resolveLocationToRegion(
  text: string | null | undefined
): { code: string; name: string; lat: number; lng: number } | null {
  if (!text?.trim()) return null
  const code = getRegionFromCity(text.trim())
  if (!code) return null
  const region = FILM_REGIONS[code]
  if (!region) return null
  return { code, name: region.name, lat: region.latitudeCenter, lng: region.longitudeCenter }
}

// Ordered list of regions for dropdowns â€” sorted by province then name
export const FILM_REGION_LIST = Object.values(FILM_REGIONS).sort((a, b) => {
  if (a.provinceCode !== b.provinceCode) return a.provinceCode.localeCompare(b.provinceCode)
  return a.name.localeCompare(b.name)
})

export function unionBadge(unionStatus: string | null | undefined): string {
  if (!unionStatus) return 'âš«'
  const s = unionStatus.toLowerCase()
  if (s.includes('full')) return 'ðŸ‘‘'
  if (s.includes('apprentice')) return 'â­'
  if (s.includes('background') || s.includes('aabp') || s.includes('permit') || s.includes('additional-bg')) return 'ðŸŸ¢'
  return 'âš«'
}

export function unionTierLabel(unionStatus: string | null | undefined): string {
  if (!unionStatus) return 'Non-Union'
  const s = unionStatus.toLowerCase()
  if (s.includes('full')) return 'Full Member'
  if (s.includes('apprentice')) return 'Apprentice'
  if (s.includes('background') || s.includes('aabp') || s.includes('additional-bg')) return 'BG Member'
  if (s.includes('permit')) return 'Permit'
  return 'Non-Union'
}

export function unionPriority(unionStatus: string | null | undefined): number {
  if (!unionStatus) return 4
  const s = unionStatus.toLowerCase()
  if (s.includes('full')) return 1
  if (s.includes('apprentice')) return 2
  if (s.includes('background') || s.includes('aabp') || s.includes('permit') || s.includes('additional-bg')) return 3
  return 4
}

**Lesson 1.1: The 24-Hour Clock System â€“ Professional Time Management**

The film industry operates on a 24-hour clock (military time) to eliminate AM/PM confusion during overnight shoots. Productions routinely run 14-16 hour days, and a 12-hour turnaround is the minimum legal requirement under ACTRA/UBCP collective agreements.

> â° **Critical Knowledge**: "Call time" is when you must be *ready to work* in wardrobe and makeup, not when you arrive. Professional performers arrive 15-20 minutes before call time to account for parking, wardrobe checks, and sign-in procedures.

**Professional Time Calculations:**
â€¢ Time is tracked in 6-minute increments (0.1 hours) for payroll accuracy
â€¢ Example: 8 hours 12 minutes = 8.2 hours on your voucher
â€¢ **Overtime Calculation**:
  - Hours 1-8: Straight time
  - Hours 8-12: 1.5x (time and a half)
  - Hours 12+: 2x (double time)
â€¢ **Meal Penalty**: If meal is delayed beyond 6 hours, production pays penalties

**Time Conversion Practice:**
| 24-Hour | Standard | 24-Hour | Standard |
|---------|----------|---------|----------|
| 0545 | 5:45 AM | 1430 | 2:30 PM |
| 0730 | 7:30 AM | 1645 | 4:45 PM |
| 1245 | 12:45 PM | 2115 | 9:15 PM |

The Assistant Director (AD) department is your primary point of contact.

| Position | Responsibility | Interaction with Background |
|----------|----------------|------------------------------|
| **1st AD** | Sets the pace, manages the schedule, ensures safety | Gives general direction to entire set |
| **2nd AD** | Creates call sheets, manages cast logistics | *Your primary contact*; handles vouchers |
| **3rd AD** | Coordinates background action, crowd scenes | *Your direct supervisor*; gives specific direction |
| **Background Wrangler** | Dedicated background handling | Manages holding area, coordinates movement |

**Professional Protocol:**
â€¢ **Never approach the Director directly** â€“ all communication flows through the AD department
â€¢ **Listen for AD instructions** â€“ they may call your name or group
â€¢ **Ask questions to 3rd AD or Background Wrangler** â€“ they are your designated contacts

**Lesson 2.1: Professional Mark Technique â€“ The Foundation of Camera Awareness**

A "mark" is a piece of colored spike tape on the floor indicating your exact position for camera focus and lighting.

**Types of Marks:**
â€¢ **T-shaped tape**: Where your feet should be placed
â€¢ **Cross-shaped tape**: Precise stop points
â€¢ **Colored tape**: Different colors for different performers

**Professional Mark Technique â€“ The 5-Step Process:**
1. **Acknowledge your mark** â€“ know where it is before blocking
2. **Feel it with your feet** â€“ use peripheral vision
3. **Count steps** â€“ memorize your stride count
4. **Use landmarks** â€“ corner of a table, edge of a rug
5. **Land softly** â€“ stop without jarring movement

> ðŸŽ¯ **Professional Insight**: Actors who consistently miss their marks are often not rehired.

> ðŸ“Œ **Note for Background Performers**: Typically, there are no marks for background performers. Do your best to remember your start and end positions.

---

**Lesson 2.2: Continuity and Matching â€“ The Invisible Art**

Continuity errors are among the most common mistakes. The Continuity Supervisor documents every detail.

**What Must Match Across Every Take:**
â€¢ **Physical positioning**: Same spot, same hand placement
â€¢ **Prop handling**: Same hand, same timing
â€¢ **Walking path**: Same route, same speed
â€¢ **Facial expression**: Same level of engagement
â€¢ **Clothing**: Same adjustments

---

â€¢ *"Eyeline camera left"* â€“ Look to the left of camera
â€¢ *"Eyeline camera right"* â€“ Look to the right of camera
â€¢ *"Eyeline to principal"* â€“ Look at the principal actor

> ðŸ‘ï¸ **Critical Rule**: **Never look directly at the camera unless specifically instructed.**

**Sightline â€“ The Camera's View**
â€¢ Don't stand between camera and principal actor

> ðŸ’¡ **Mistakes Happen**: If you've done something incorrect, don't worry. The 3rd AD or Wrangler will tell you to try something else.

> ðŸš¨ **Heat Stroke Protocol (4 Steps):**
> 1. **Call 911 immediately** â€” heat stroke is a life-threatening emergency
> 2. **Notify the 1st AD and Set Medic immediately**
> 3. **Move the person to a cool, shaded area** â€” out of direct sun

| Stage | Symptoms | Action |
|-------|----------|--------|

**Lesson 5.2: ACTRA/UBCP â€“ Professional Union Membership**

**Union Membership Levels (UBCP/ACTRA):**

| Level | Requirements | Benefits |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| **Background (BG) Union Member** | Work as a union background performer | Higher pay than non-union; union protection; counts toward apprenticeship |
| **Apprentice Union Member** | Accumulated union background vouchers (varies by region) | All BG benefits + ability to audition for speaking roles; progress toward full membership |
| **Full Union Member** | 3 qualifying credits (speaking/featured roles) + initiation fee ($1,600 CAD) | Full benefits including pension (7%), health benefits, residuals, voting rights, legal representation |

**Benefits of ACTRA/UBCP Union Membership:**
â€¢ Higher pay
â€¢ Pension contributions (7%)
â€¢ Health benefits
â€¢ Residuals
â€¢ Workplace protections
â€¢ Legal representation

**How to Become a Member:**
â€¢ Start as a non-union background performer
â€¢ Get hired on union vouchers to become BG Union Member
â€¢ Accumulate credits to become Apprentice
â€¢ 3 qualifying credits (speaking/featured roles) required for full membership
â€¢ Background permits do NOT count toward full membership â€“ only speaking/featured roles
â€¢ ACTRA/UBCP initiation fee: $1,600 CAD

  6: `Foundation

**Source:** *An Actor Prepares* (1936), *Building a Character* (1949), *Creating a Role* (1961) by Konstantin Stanislavski

---

**Lesson 6.1: Given Circumstances â€“ The Actor's Foundation**

The "given circumstances" are all the conditions of the character's life that the actor must accept as true and real. This includes the plot, facts, events, time, place, and social conditions of the play.

| Category | Questions to Ask |
|----------|------------------|
| *Who you are* | Name, age, occupation, social status, education, family background |
| *Where you are* | Location, city, country, room, time period, historical context |
| *When it is* | Year, season, time of day, historical era, political climate |
| *What you want* | Immediate objective, long-term goals, deepest desires |
| *What just happened* | The moment before the scene began |
| *Your relationships* | To other characters, history, power dynamics, emotional connections |

**Additional Context from Stanislavski:**
- Given circumstances are the "facts" of the play that cannot be changed
- They provide the foundation upon which the actor builds truth
- The more specific and detailed your given circumstances, the more truthful your performance

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "The more specific your given circumstances, the more truthful your performance."

---

**Lesson 6.2: The Magic If â€“ The Actor's Imagination**

Ask yourself: **"What would I do IF I were in this character's situation?"**

**How to Use the Magic If:**
1. Read the scene thoroughly, noting all given circumstances
2. Identify the character's situation and challenges
3. Set aside personal judgment of the character
4. Ask yourself honestly what *you* would do in their place
5. Allow your genuine, truthful response to emerge
6. Let the "if" trigger your imagination without forcing

**Purpose:**
- Bridges the gap between the actor's reality and the character's circumstances
- Activates the imagination rather than intellectual analysis
- Creates organic, believable behavior

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "The magic if starts the creative process."

---

**Lesson 6.3: Objectives â€“ What Your Character Wants**

An **objective** (also called a "goal" or "intention") is what the character wants or needs to achieve in a scene or unit of action.

**Strong Objectives Must Be:**

| Criteria | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| *Active* | Uses action verbs (to persuade, to threaten, to seduce) | "To make him admit the truth" |
| *Specific* | Clear and concrete, not vague | "To get her phone number" not "To connect" |
| *Personal* | Connected to the character's own desires and stakes | What do *they* stand to gain or lose? |
| *Urgent* | Must happen now; has immediacy and pressure | "Before he walks out that door" |
| *Playable* | You can actively do it to another person | "To embarrass him" is playable; "To feel sad" is not |

**Weak vs Strong Objectives:**

| âŒ Weak (Emotion-based) | âœ… Strong (Action-based) |
|------------------------|--------------------------|
| To be happy | To make him laugh |
| To be liked | To earn her respect |
| To win | To prove I'm right |
| To feel loved | To get her to say "I love you" |
| To be angry | To punish him for betraying me |

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "An objective must be clear, logical, and active."

---

**Lesson 6.4: Subtext â€“ The Meaning Beneath the Words**

**Subtext** is the underlying meaning, thought, or emotion beneath the spoken words. It is what the character *really* means but may not say directly.

**Understanding Subtext:**
- Dialogue is often what people *say*; subtext is what they *mean*
- Characters may lie, hide feelings, or be unable to express themselves directly
- The audience senses the subtext even when words say something else

**Examples of Subtext:**

| Spoken Line | Possible Subtext |
|-------------|------------------|
| "I'm fine." | "I'm hurt and you should know why." |
| "Congratulations." | "I'm jealous and bitter." |
| "We should talk sometime." | "I never want to see you again." |
| "Thank you for everything." | "You've ruined my life." |

**How to Find Subtext:**
1. Ask: What is the character *really* trying to communicate?
2. Ask: What are they afraid to say directly?
3. Ask: What do they want the other person to feel or do?
4. Play the subtext, not just the line

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "The text is the surface; the subtext is the depth."

---

**Lesson 6.5: The Super Objective â€“ The Character's Life Goal**

The **super objective** is the character's overarching life goal that drives all scene objectives across the entire play. It is the through-line connecting every action the character takes.

**Finding the Super Objective:**
1. Read the entire script from beginning to end
2. Notice what the character talks about repeatedly
3. Identify what drives their actions across multiple scenes
4. Look for what they ultimately want more than anything
5. Express it in active, human, one-sentence terms

**Examples of Super Objectives:**

| Character | Super Objective |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Hamlet | To expose the truth about his father's murder |
| Willy Loman | To leave behind something of value for his sons |
| Nora (A Doll's House) | To discover who she truly is outside of marriage |
| Blanche (Streetcar) | To find protection from the harsh realities of life |

**Common Super Objectives:**
- To be loved unconditionally
- To achieve power and control
- To find truth and justice
- To protect my family at all costs
- To prove my worth to the world
- To escape my past

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "The super objective is the main goal toward which all the smaller objectives strive."

---

**Lesson 6.6: The Through-Line of Action â€“ Connecting the Journey**

The **through-line of action** (also called the "through-line" or "spine") is the continuous line of objectives that runs from the beginning of the play to the end. It connects every unit, scene, and act into a single, unbroken chain of purposeful action.

**How the Through-Line Works:**
- Each scene objective serves the super objective
- Each unit of action leads logically to the next
- The character's journey has direction and purpose
- No action is arbitrary or disconnected

**Example from A Streetcar Named Desire (Blanche):**
| Scene Objective | Serves Super Objective |
|-----------------|------------------------|
| To impress Stella's friends | To appear desirable and refined |
| To flirt with Mitch | To secure a protector |
| To hide her past | To avoid rejection |
| To attack Stanley's cruelty | To defend her dignity |

All serve the **super objective**: To find protection from harsh reality.

**Mapping Your Through-Line:**
1. Identify your character's super objective
2. Break the script into units of action (beats)
3. Name each unit's objective in active terms
4. Trace how each objective connects to the next
5. Ensure every action serves the through-line

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "The through-line of action is what gives the play its meaning and direction."

---

**Lesson 6.7: The Danger of Playing Emotions Directly**

One of Stanislavski's most important insights: **You cannot play an emotion directly. You can only play actions, and emotion will follow.**

**Why Playing Emotions Fails:**
- Emotions are results, not actions
- Trying to "be angry" leads to clichÃ©d, generalized performances
- Direct emotion-playing looks fake and theatrical
- Audiences sense when emotion is manufactured rather than experienced

**Examples of Mistaken Approaches:**

| âŒ Playing Emotion | âœ… Playing Action |
|-------------------|-------------------|
| "I'm going to be sad" | "To mourn the loss" |
| "I'll show anger" | "To punish him for hurting me" |
| "Make the audience cry" | "To make her feel my pain" |
| "Be happy" | "To celebrate my good fortune" |

**The Correct Approach:**
1. Focus on what your character *wants* (objective)
2. Choose *actions* to achieve that objective
3. Pursue those actions truthfully
4. Allow emotions to arise organically as a *result*

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "Never try to play feelings. They will appear of their own accord as a result of your actions."

---

**Lesson 6.8: Method of Physical Actions â€“ Action Creates Emotion**

The **Method of Physical Actions** was Stanislavski's later refinement of his system. It emphasizes that physical actions are the most reliable pathway to emotional truth.

**Core Principle:** Physical actions are easier to control than emotions. If you perform the correct physical actions truthfully, the correct emotions will follow naturally.

**How to Use the Method of Physical Actions:**
1. Identify what your character *does* in the scene
2. Break down the physical actions step by step
3. Perform the actions with full concentration and truth
4. Let the emotional life emerge from the physical reality

**Example:**
Instead of trying to "feel nervous," you might:
- Fidget with a ring
- Check your watch repeatedly
- Straighten papers that don't need straightening
- Pace to the window and back

The physical actions *produce* the feeling of nervousness.

**Why This Works:**
- Physical actions are voluntary and controllable
- Emotions are involuntary and uncontrollable
- The mind-body connection works both ways
- Truthful action leads to truthful emotion

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "The most difficult thing is to get hold of the evasive, capricious, and stubborn emotions. But physical actions are easier to deal with."

---

**Lesson 6.9: Public Solitude â€“ Performing While Private**

**Public solitude** (Russian: *Ð¿ÑƒÐ±Ð»Ð¸Ñ‡Ð½Ð¾Ðµ Ð¾Ð´Ð¸Ð½Ð¾Ñ‡ÐµÑÑ‚Ð²Ð¾*) is the ability to maintain complete private concentration while performing in front of an audience or camera crew.

**The Paradox:**
- You must be aware of your technique (hitting marks, camera angles)
- Yet you must appear completely unaware of being watched
- You are alone in public

**How to Achieve Public Solitude:**
1. Focus entirely on your partner and the given circumstances
2. Create such specific, detailed inner life that you forget the audience
3. Let your attention be absorbed by your character's objectives
4. The "fourth wall" exists to help you ignore the watchers

**Common Blocks to Public Solitude:**
- Self-consciousness ("How do I look?")
- Audience awareness ("Are they watching me?")
- Technical preoccupation ("Did I hit my mark?")
- Judgment ("Is this good enough?")

**Solutions:**
- Prepare so thoroughly that technique becomes automatic
- Redirect attention to your partner and objectives
- Trust your training and let go

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "The actor must learn to be alone in public."

---

**Lesson 6.10: Tempo-Rhythm â€“ The Inner and Outer Pace**

**Tempo-rhythm** refers to the combination of inner emotional pace (tempo) and outer physical rhythm (rhythm) that reveals a character's emotional state.

**Two Components:**

| Component | Definition | Examples |
|-----------|------------|----------|
| *Tempo* | Speed or pace of inner emotional life | Rushing panic, slow depression |
| *Rhythm* | Pattern or flow of physical movement | Jerky anxiety, smooth confidence |

**How Tempo-Rhythm Reveals Character:**
- Fast tempo + sharp rhythm = Panic, anger, excitement
- Slow tempo + smooth rhythm = Depression, exhaustion, peace
- Fast tempo + smooth rhythm = Joy, enthusiasm
- Slow tempo + sharp rhythm = Suspicion, calculation

**Using Tempo-Rhythm in Performance:**
1. Identify the character's emotional state
2. Find the appropriate tempo (inner speed)
3. Find the appropriate rhythm (outer pattern)
4. Let both change naturally as the scene progresses
5. Use tempo-rhythm changes to mark beat shifts

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "Tempo-rhythm is the heartbeat of the scene."

---

**Lesson 6.11: Relaxation â€“ Releasing Unnecessary Tension**

**Relaxation** (also called "muscle release") is the removal of physical tension that blocks truthful expression and free movement.

**Why Tension is the Enemy:**
- Physical tension creates false, stiff performances
- Tension blocks emotional availability
- Audiences sense tension as "acting" rather than "being"
- Tension leads to vocal strain and physical limitation

**Common Tension Areas:**
- Jaw and throat (affects voice)
- Shoulders and neck (affects breathing)
- Lower back (affects movement)
- Hands and fingers (affects gestures)

**Relaxation Exercises:**
1. Progressive muscle release (tense then release each muscle group)
2. Alexander Technique (head-neck-back alignment)
3. Breath work (exhale fully to release holding patterns)
4. Physical warm-ups before rehearsal and performance

**Daily Practice:**
- Check in with your body throughout the day
- Notice where you hold unnecessary tension
- Release consciously and repeatedly
- Build awareness until relaxation becomes habit

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "Tension is the enemy of truth. Relaxation is the first step toward creative freedom."

---

**Lesson 6.12: Emotional Memory â€“ Accessing Authentic Emotion**

**Emotional memory** (also called "affective memory") is recalling your own past experiences to generate authentic emotion that can be channeled into the character's circumstances.

**The Process:**
1. Recall a specific past event with similar emotional qualities to the scene
2. Relive it using sensory details (sights, sounds, smells, textures)
3. Let the emotion arise naturally without forcing it
4. Channel that emotion into the character's circumstances
5. Use the emotion, not the memory itself

**Guidelines for Safe Practice:**
- Never use traumatic memories that could harm your psychological health
- Work with a teacher or coach when exploring intense material
- Have a "recovery" practice to return to neutral after emotional work
- If a memory is too painful, choose a different one

**Alternatives to Emotional Memory:**
- The Magic If (imagination-based)
- Method of Physical Actions (action-based)
- Substitution (replacing circumstances with personal equivalents)

âš ï¸ **Warning**: Stanislavski himself later moderated his emphasis on emotional memory after seeing students harm themselves with traumatic material. Use with care and professional guidance.

ðŸ“– **Stanislavski**: "Emotional memory is like a dreamâ€”you cannot force it. You can only create the conditions for it to arise."

---

## Module 6 Summary â€“ Key Concepts

| Concept | Definition |
|---------|------------|
| **Given Circumstances** | The facts of the play (who, what, where, when, why) |
| **Magic If** | "What would I do if I were in this situation?" |
| **Objective** | What the character wants in a scene |
| **Subtext** | The meaning beneath the spoken words |
| **Super Objective** | The character's overarching life goal |
| **Through-Line of Action** | The continuous chain of objectives through the play |
| **Playing Actions (not emotions)** | Pursue objectives; emotions follow naturally |
| **Method of Physical Actions** | Physical actions are the pathway to emotional truth |
| **Public Solitude** | Private concentration in public performance |
| **Tempo-Rhythm** | Inner speed and outer pattern of movement |
| **Relaxation** | Release of tension that blocks truth |
| **Emotional Memory** | Recalling past experiences for authentic emotion |

ðŸ“– **Required Reading:** *An Actor Prepares* by Konstantin Stanislavski, Chapters 1-8`,

  7: `Audition Technique

**Source:** *Audition: Everything an Actor Needs to Know to Get the Part* by Michael Shurtleff (1978)

---

**Lesson 7.1: The 12 Guideposts â€“ Overview**

Michael Shurtleff, one of Broadway's most legendary casting directors, developed the **12 Guideposts** â€“ a systematic approach to script analysis and audition preparation that has been used by actors for decades.

**The 12 Guideposts:**

| # | Guidepost | Core Question |
|---|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Relationship** | Who are these people to each other? |
| 2 | **Conflict** | What are they fighting for? |
| 3 | **The Moment Before** | What just happened? |
| 4 | **Humor** | What is funny in this scene? |
| 5 | **Opposites** | What is the unexpected choice? |
| 6 | **Discoveries** | What is new in each moment? |
| 7 | **Communication and Competition** | Who is winning? |
| 8 | **Importance** | Why does this matter? |
| 9 | **Find the Events** | What changes? |
| 10 | **Place** | Where are we? |
| 11 | **Game Playing and Role Playing** | Who is pretending? |
| 12 | **Mystery and Secret** | What is unsaid? |

**How to Use the Guideposts:**
- Apply them in order (1 through 12)
- Each guidepost builds on the previous ones
- They work for scenes, monologues, and entire plays
- They are not rules â€“ they are questions to ask

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "The guideposts are not something you memorize. They are something you *use*."

---

**Lesson 7.2: Guidepost 1 â€“ Relationship**

**Relationship** is the most important guidepost. Without knowing who these people are to each other, you cannot play the scene truthfully.

**Why Relationship Comes First:**
- Every line is spoken *to* someone
- How you speak depends on who you're speaking to
- The same line changes completely based on relationship
- Relationship determines power, intimacy, and history

**Questions to Ask About Relationship:**

| Question | Example |
|----------|---------|
| What is the history between these characters? | "We were lovers ten years ago" |
| How do they feel about each other? | "She still resents him" |
| What do they need from each other? | "He needs her forgiveness" |
| What is the power dynamic? | "She has all the power now" |
| What is unspoken between them? | "Neither will mention the affair" |
| How has the relationship changed? | "It changed when he left" |

**The Relationship Spectrum:**
- Stranger â†’ Acquaintance â†’ Friend â†’ Lover â†’ Family â†’ Enemy
- Each level changes how you speak, stand, and listen
- Relationships are rarely simple (lovers who are also rivals, family who are also strangers)

**Applying Relationship to a Line:**
- "I need to talk to you" to a boss = nervous, deferential
- "I need to talk to you" to a child = firm, parental
- "I need to talk to you" to a lover = intimate, urgent
- "I need to talk to you" to an enemy = threatening, cold

ðŸŽ­ **Shurtleff**: "Relationship is the most important guidepost. Get it wrong, and nothing else matters."

---

**Lesson 7.3: Guidepost 2 â€“ Conflict**

**Conflict** is the heart of drama. Conflict is not fighting â€“ it's opposing wants. Without conflict, there is no scene.

**What Conflict Is:**
- Two characters want different things
- Both cannot get what they want
- They try to change each other
- The scene ends when one wins, loses, or compromises

**What Conflict Is NOT:**
- Physical violence (though that can be a tactic)
- Yelling or arguing (though that can happen)
- Disagreement (disagreement without stakes is not conflict)
- Anger (anger is an emotion, not conflict)

**Questions to Ask About Conflict:**

| Question | Example |
|----------|---------|
| What does each character want? | "He wants her to stay; she wants to leave" |
| How are those wants in opposition? | "They cannot both get what they want" |
| Who is winning at each moment? | "She is winning until he reveals the secret" |
| What tactics are they using? | "She uses guilt; he uses promises" |
| What happens if they lose? | "If she stays, she loses her freedom; if he loses her, he's alone" |

**Conflict and Stakes:**
- The higher the stakes, the stronger the conflict
- Stakes are what the character stands to gain or lose
- Stakes must be specific and personal
- "Losing" must matter deeply to the character

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "No conflict, no scene. Find the opposing wants and play them."

---

**Lesson 7.4: Guidepost 3 â€“ The Moment Before**

**The Moment Before** is what happened immediately before the scene began. It gives you emotional preparation and a reason for being in the scene.

**Why The Moment Before Matters:**
- Characters don't start from neutral
- You carry the previous moment into the scene
- The first line is a response to what just happened
- The audience senses what came before

**Questions to Ask About The Moment Before:**

| Question | Example |
|----------|---------|
| Where was I just before the scene started? | "I was in the kitchen making dinner" |
| What was I doing? | "I was chopping vegetables, crying" |
| How was I feeling? | "I was furious and hurt" |
| What am I bringing into this scene? | "I'm bringing the knife I was holding" |
| What interrupted me? | "I heard him come in" |
| How did I get here? | "I walked from the kitchen, still holding the knife" |

**The Moment Before in Different Contexts:**

| Entrance | Possible Moment Before |
|----------|------------------------|
| Entering a party | You just got great news and are buzzing with excitement |
| Entering a job interview | You just threw up in the bathroom from nerves |
| Answering a phone call | You were praying they would call |
| Starting a scene at a table | You've been waiting for 20 minutes, getting angrier |

**How to Use The Moment Before:**
1. Create a specific, detailed moment before
2. Let it affect your physical state (heart rate, breathing, tension)
3. Enter the scene carrying that emotional and physical reality
4. Let the first moment of the scene be a response to what just happened

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "The moment before is what you're bringing into the room. Without it, you're just starting from nowhere."

---

**Lesson 7.5: Guidepost 4 â€“ Humor**

**Humor** is finding what is funny in every scene â€“ even dramas, even tragedies. Not jokes, but the human, ironic, absurd moments that make us laugh.

**Why Humor Belongs in Every Scene:**
- Life has humor even in dark moments
- Humor provides relief and contrast
- The darkest scenes often have the most ironic humor
- Characters use humor as a defense, a weapon, or a connection

**What Humor Is NOT:**
- Telling jokes
- Being a comedian
- Playing for laughs
- Undermining the drama

**Finding Humor in Any Scene:**

| Type of Humor | Example |
|---------------|---------|
| *Ironic* | A character swears they'll never fall in love again â€“ then immediately meets someone |
| *Absurd* | A funeral where everyone is trying not to laugh |
| *Defensive* | A character makes a joke to hide their pain |
| *Character-based* | A pompous character's self-importance is funny to everyone but them |
| *Situational* | The worst possible thing happens at the worst possible time |

**How to Play Humor:**
- Don't play for laughs â€“ play the situation truthfully
- Trust that if the situation is funny, the audience will laugh
- Let the humor arise from character and circumstance
- Even in comedy, play the reality, not the joke

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "Find the humor in everything. It's there. If you don't see it, you're not looking hard enough."

---

**Lesson 7.6: Guidepost 5 â€“ Opposites**

**Opposites** means playing the opposite of what is expected. The most interesting choices are often the unexpected ones.

**The Principle:**
- If the line says "I love you," find the opposite ("I'm terrified of losing you")
- If the scene is sad, find what's hopeful
- If the character is powerful, find their weakness
- The truth is rarely one-dimensional

**Examples of Opposites:**

| Expected Choice | Opposite Choice (More Interesting) |
|-----------------|-------------------------------------|
| Angry shouting | Cold, quiet menace |
| Romantic confession | Awkward, embarrassed admission |
| Triumphant victory | Exhausted, hollow relief |
| Grief-stricken sobbing | Numb, disconnected shock |
| Confident seduction | Vulnerable, hopeful reaching |

**Why Opposites Work:**
- Humans are contradictory
- We hide what we feel
- We say one thing and mean another
- The opposite choice reveals the subtext

**How to Find the Opposite:**
1. Identify the obvious, expected choice
2. Ask: "What is the opposite of that?"
3. Ask: "Could the character be hiding the opposite?"
4. Try the opposite in rehearsal
5. Let the scene tell you if it works

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "If you think you know how to play a scene, play the opposite. You'll be surprised."

---

**Lesson 7.7: Guidepost 6 â€“ Discoveries**

**Discoveries** are what is new in each moment. Acting is reacting to discoveries. The scene should be happening for the first time, every time.

**What Discoveries Are:**
- New information that changes the character's understanding
- A shift in the other person's behavior
- Realizing something about yourself
- Seeing something you missed before

**What Discoveries Are NOT:**
- Planned reactions
- Pre-decided emotions
- Performing what you already know

**Types of Discoveries:**

| Type | Example |
|------|---------|
| *Factual* | "Wait â€“ you're the one who took the money?" |
| *Emotional* | "I just realized I still love you" |
| *Relational* | "You've been lying to me this whole time?" |
| *Self-discovery* | "I'm not the person I thought I was" |
| *Physical* | "There's something behind that door" |

**How to Play Discoveries:**
- Don't anticipate what's coming
- Listen to your partner as if you've never heard the lines before
- Let yourself be surprised by what happens
- Allow your responses to be spontaneous
- Each take should feel like the first time

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "If you know what's going to happen, you're not acting. You're reciting."

---

**Lesson 7.8: Guidepost 7 â€“ Communication and Competition**

**Communication and Competition** tracks who is winning moment to moment. Every scene is a competition where characters try to get what they want.

**Two Aspects of This Guidepost:**

| Aspect | Definition |
|--------|------------|
| *Communication* | Are you actually reaching your partner? Are they hearing you? |
| *Competition* | Who is winning right now? Who has the upper hand? |

**Tracking the Competition:**
- Power shifts constantly in a scene
- One character gains advantage, then the other
- The competition is for who gets what they want
- Winning and losing can happen multiple times in a scene

**Signs of Winning vs. Losing:**

| Winning | Losing |
|---------|--------|
| Your partner responds to you | Your partner ignores or dismisses you |
| You get what you want | You don't get what you want |
| Your partner changes behavior | You change your behavior |
| You feel empowered | You feel frustrated |

**How to Play Communication and Competition:**
1. Know what you want (objective)
2. Try to get it (actions)
3. Notice if you're winning or losing
4. Change tactics when you're losing
5. Press your advantage when you're winning

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "Acting is a contact sport. You're trying to change the other person. They're trying to change you."

---

**Lesson 7.9: Guidepost 8 â€“ Importance**

**Importance** means raising the stakes. Make it matter more. If it's not important to the character, it won't be important to the audience.

**The Principle:**
- Characters don't do things that don't matter to them
- The higher the stakes, the more compelling the scene
- "Importance" is what's at risk
- Every scene should feel like life or death (to the character)

**Questions to Raise Importance:**

| Question | Example |
|----------|---------|
| What does the character stand to lose? | "If she leaves, I lose everything" |
| What happens if they fail? | "I'll be alone for the rest of my life" |
| Why does this have to happen now? | "This is my last chance" |
| What's the best possible outcome? | "We could finally be happy" |
| What's the worst possible outcome? | "I'll never forgive myself" |

**Escalating Importance:**
- Start with what's immediately at stake
- Ask "What's the worst that could happen?"
- Ask "And then what?"
- Keep going until you reach the character's deepest fear or desire
- Play the highest possible stakes

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "If it's not important to you, it won't be important to us. Raise the stakes."

---

**Lesson 7.10: Guidepost 9 â€“ Find the Events**

**Events** are moments where something changes â€“ a shift in power, new information, a decision, an action that changes everything.

**What an Event Is:**
- A turning point in the scene
- Something that happens that changes the direction
- The moment after which nothing is the same
- The "what just happened" that creates a new beat

**Examples of Events:**

| Event | What Changes |
|-------|--------------|
| A secret is revealed | Everything is seen differently now |
| Someone enters or leaves | The dynamic shifts completely |
| A decision is made | The scene moves in a new direction |
| A line is crossed | There's no going back |
| Someone wins or loses | The competition is over |

**How to Find Events:**
1. Read the scene and mark every moment of change
2. Ask: "What happens that makes things different?"
3. Ask: "What is the turning point?"
4. Note where the characters' objectives shift
5. Each event begins a new beat

**Playing Events:**
- Events are not just intellectual â€“ they affect you physically
- Let events land on you (don't anticipate them)
- The audience should feel the event happen
- After an event, nothing is the same

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "Find the events. They are the skeleton of the scene. Without them, there is no structure."

---

**Lesson 7.11: Guidepost 10 â€“ Place**

**Place** is not just a location â€“ it's the specific, sensory, emotional reality of where you are. Be specific. A park bench isn't just wood â€“ it's where you got engaged, or where you were abandoned.

**Why Place Matters:**
- Where you are affects how you behave
- Different places have different rules, sounds, smells, and temperatures
- Place creates mood, memory, and meaning
- The audience senses when the place is real to you

**Questions to Make Place Specific:**

| Question | Example |
|----------|---------|
| Where exactly am I? | "The third booth from the window at Joe's Diner" |
| What does it look like? | "Red vinyl seats, a jukebox, neon sign buzzing" |
| What does it smell like? | "Coffee, grease, stale cigarette smoke" |
| What does it sound like? | "Clinking plates, the fryer hissing, a waitress yelling orders" |
| What does it feel like? | "Sticky vinyl, a draft from the door" |
| What does this place mean to my character? | "This is where he proposed. Now I'm here to tell him it's over." |

**Place in Different Contexts:**

| Place | Emotional Meaning |
|-------|-------------------|
| A hospital waiting room | Fear, hope, boredom, dread |
| A childhood bedroom | Nostalgia, resentment, safety, entrapment |
| A job interview office | Anxiety, ambition, desperation, confidence |
| A subway platform | Impatience, anonymity, danger, possibility |

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "A park bench is not just a park bench. It's where she said goodbye. It's where he sat every Sunday. It's where the pigeons gather. Be specific."

---

**Lesson 7.12: Guidepost 11 â€“ Game Playing and Role Playing**

**Game Playing and Role Playing** recognizes that people rarely present their true selves. We play roles: victim, hero, innocent, seducer, fool. We play games: "Poor me," "I'm just trying to help," "You owe me."

**What This Guidepost Reveals:**
- Characters are rarely authentic
- We hide our true intentions behind roles
- We play games to get what we want
- The "real" character is often hidden beneath the role

**Common Roles People Play:**

| Role | What It Hides |
|------|---------------|
| *Victim* | Manipulation, control, avoidance of responsibility |
| *Hero* | Insecurity, need for approval, fear of failure |
| *Innocent* | Guilt, knowledge, complicity |
| *Seducer* | Loneliness, desperation, need for validation |
| *Jester* | Pain, fear, avoidance of real connection |
| *Martyr* | Resentment, need for recognition, control |

**Common Games People Play:**

| Game | Real Objective |
|------|----------------|
| "Ain't it awful" | To get sympathy without taking action |
| "I'm just trying to help" | To interfere or control |
| "See what you made me do" | To avoid responsibility |
| "Poor me" | To gain attention and service |
| "Now I've got you" | To trap or expose someone |

**How to Play Game Playing and Role Playing:**
1. Identify the role the character is playing
2. Identify what they're hiding
3. Play the role truthfully (the character believes it)
4. Let the mask slip when pressure is applied
5. The audience should see both the role and the truth beneath

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "Everyone is playing a game. Find the game. Play the game. Then find what's underneath."

---

**Lesson 7.13: Guidepost 12 â€“ Mystery and Secret**

**Mystery and Secret** is what isn't being said â€“ often more important than what is said. The unspoken haunts the scene.

**What Mystery and Secret Means:**
- Characters hide things
- The audience senses what's hidden
- Mystery creates tension and curiosity
- Secrets create subtext

**Types of Mystery and [[REDACTED]]

| Type | Example |
|------|---------|
| *A secret the character knows* | "I'm the one who stole the money" |
| *A secret the character doesn't know* | "He's been lying to you for years" |
| *A mystery the audience wants solved* | "Who is calling on the phone?" |
| *A hidden emotion* | "I love you but I can't say it" |
| *A hidden agenda* | "I'm not really here to help you" |

**How to Play Mystery and [[REDACTED]]
- Don't telegraph the secret (don't "act mysterious")
- Play the character's attempt to hide
- Let the pressure of the secret affect you
- The audience should feel something is underneath
- The revelation is more powerful when the hiding was real

**Mystery vs. [[REDACTED]]

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| *Mystery* | Something the audience wants to discover along with the character |
| *Secret* | Something the character knows but is hiding from others (or themselves) |

ðŸ“– **Shurtleff**: "What isn't being said is often the whole scene. Find the mystery. Find the secret."

---

**Lesson 7.14: Audition Practical Application**

The 12 Guideposts are not theoretical â€“ they are practical tools for audition preparation and performance.

**Before the Audition (Preparation):**

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Read the sides 3-5 times for overall meaning |
| 2 | Apply all 12 guideposts to the scene |
| 3 | Make specific, playable choices |
| 4 | Know your objective and actions |
| 5 | Prepare your moment before |
| 6 | Research the show if possible |
| 7 | Arrive early, relaxed, and ready |

**During the Audition:**

| Do | Don't |
|----|-------|
| Make eye contact with your partner | Look at the casting director while acting |
| Use the space (within reason) | Wander aimlessly |
| Show range (quiet and loud, still and active) | Stay in one emotional key |
| Take direction (show you can adjust) | Argue with or question the direction |
| Listen and respond truthfully | Recite lines you've memorized |
| Let yourself be affected | Stay neutral and "professional" |

**The Shurtleff Mantra for Auditions:**
"The audition is not about getting the job. It's about doing your work."

**Why This Mindset Matters:**
- Desperation reads as desperation
- When you focus on the work, you relax
- Casting directors want you to succeed
- Your job is to serve the scene, not impress the room
- The best auditions happen when you forget you're auditioning

**After the Audition:**
- Thank the casting director (briefly)
- Leave professionally (don't linger)
- Let it go completely (don't obsess)
- Move on to the next opportunity

ðŸŽ¬ **Shurtleff**: "The audition is not about getting the job. It's about doing your work. If you do your work, you might get the job. If you focus on getting the job, you won't do your work."

---

## Module 7 Summary â€“ The 12 Guideposts

| # | Guidepost | Core Question | Key Insight |
|---|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Relationship** | Who are these people to each other? | Most important guidepost |
| 2 | **Conflict** | What are they fighting for? | Opposing wants, not fighting |
| 3 | **The Moment Before** | What just happened? | You carry the past into the scene |
| 4 | **Humor** | What is funny in this scene? | Find it in every scene |
| 5 | **Opposites** | What is the unexpected choice? | Play the opposite |
| 6 | **Discoveries** | What is new in each moment? | Act as if it's the first time |
| 7 | **Communication and Competition** | Who is winning? | Track the power shifts |
| 8 | **Importance** | Why does this matter? | Raise the stakes |
| 9 | **Find the Events** | What changes? | Events create structure |
| 10 | **Place** | Where are we? | Be specific and sensory |
| 11 | **Game Playing and Role Playing** | Who is pretending? | Find the mask and what's beneath |
| 12 | **Mystery and Secret** | What is unsaid? | The unspoken is the whole scene |

ðŸ“– **Required Reading:** *Audition: Everything an Actor Needs to Know to Get the Part* by Michael Shurtleff (1978)

---

**Lesson 7.15: Audition Formats â€“ Cold Reading, Monologue, and Self-Tape**

Understanding the format of an audition is as important as your preparation. Different formats demand different skills.

---

**Cold Reading**

A **cold reading** is when you receive the sides immediately before or during the audition â€” sometimes with only minutes to prepare.

**How to Prepare for a Cold Reading:**
1. **Scan the scene** â€“ Get the gist before reading line by line
2. **Identify your character's want** â€“ What do they need from this scene?
3. **Find the top of the scene** â€“ What is your character doing before the scene starts?
4. **Pick one strong choice** â€“ Commit to it. Don't hedge.
5. **Hold the page at 45Â°** â€“ So you can look up and make eye contact

**Cold Reading Tips:**

| Do | Don't |
|----|-------|
| Hold the sides â€” it's expected | Try to memorize everything in 5 minutes |
| Glance up on emotional moments | Keep your eyes buried in the page |
| Make a strong, specific choice | Play it safe and neutral |
| Listen to your reader | Rush through to prove you know the lines |
| Take a breath before starting | Jump in before you've settled |

> ðŸ“– **Pro Insight:** Casting directors know it's a cold read. They're watching *how* you make choices under pressure â€” not whether you have the lines memorized.

---

**Monologue Length**

For auditions that require a prepared monologue:

â€¢ **Target length: 60â€“90 seconds** (approximately 150â€“200 words spoken at a natural pace)
â€¢ Under 60 seconds = too short to show range
â€¢ Over 2 minutes = risks losing the casting director's attention
â€¢ For student films and non-union work: 60â€“90 seconds is universally appropriate
â€¢ For principal auditions with larger agencies: confirm the expected length in advance

**Choosing Your Monologue:**
- Contemporary is usually preferred over classical (unless casting specifically requires it)
- Choose material that suits your current age range and casting type
- Avoid overdone monologues unless you have a truly fresh take
- Pick material that gives you something to *do*, not just *feel*

---

**Self-Tape Auditions**

Self-tape is now the **most common audition format** in the Canadian film industry. Most initial auditions â€” especially for background-to-principal upgrades and principal roles â€” begin as self-tapes.

**Standard Self-Tape Format:**

| Element | Requirement |
|---------|-------------|
| Background | Neutral, uncluttered wall (grey, white, light blue) |
| Lighting | Soft front light on your face â€” no harsh shadows |
| Framing | Mid-shot: top of head to mid-chest |
| Sound | Quiet room, no echo, external mic recommended |
| Reader | Off-camera, consistent tone, not acting |
| Slating | State your name and role at the start |
| File format | MP4, under 500MB, labelled: LastName_FirstName_Role |

**Self-Tape Tips:**
â€¢ Film multiple takes and send your best â€” not your first
â€¢ Wear character-appropriate clothing (refer to Lesson 3.6 for wardrobe guidance)
â€¢ Submit same day unless given a deadline â€” early submissions are noticed
â€¢ Review your tape before sending: check sound, framing, and performance
â€¢ Do not add music, titles, or effects

> ðŸŽ¬ **Industry Reality:** A strong self-tape that arrives same-day beats a perfect tape that arrives late. Responsiveness signals professionalism.`,

  8: `Scene Study

**Source:** *Respect for Acting* (1973) by Uta Hagen, *A Challenge for the Actor* (1991)

---

**Lesson 8.1: Substitution â€“ Making It Personal**

**Substitution** is replacing the character's circumstances with your own reality to make the situation personally meaningful and emotionally truthful.

**The Core Question:**
"Who is this person to me? What would *I* feel if someone like this did this to *me*?"

**Example:**
- The character's father has died
- You substitute your own beloved pet who died
- The grief you access is real, even if the cause is different
- The truth of the emotion serves the character's situation

**Rules for Substitution:**
1. Must have **equivalent emotional weight** (don't substitute a parking ticket for a death)
2. Must have **similar relationship dynamics** (parent/child, lover/rival, friend/betrayer)
3. Must be **specific and personal** (not "someone I love" but "my grandmother")
4. Must be **appropriate to the character** (don't import your modern sensibilities into a Victorian play)
5. Must be able to access the emotion **safely** (without psychological harm)

**The Six W's (Hagen's Foundation):**

| Question | What to Ask |
|----------|-------------|
| *Who am I?* | Name, age, occupation, social status, education, temperament |
| *What am I?* | Profession, role in society, self-image |
| *Where am I?* | Specific location, city, room, time period |
| *When is it?* | Year, season, time of day, historical context |
| *What do I want?* | Immediate objective, deeper need, ultimate desire |
| *Why do I want it?* | The stakes, what I gain or lose |
| *How will I get it?* | The actions and tactics I'll use |

ðŸ“– **Hagen**: "The more personal your substitution, the more truthful your performance."

---

**Lesson 8.2: Endowment â€“ Giving Objects Meaning**

**Endowment** is giving physical objects and places specific, personal meaning and history that the character would have.

**The Principle:**
An object is not just an object. It is what it *means* to the character.

**How to Endow Objects:**
1. Choose an object or place in the scene
2. Give it a personal history (real or imagined)
3. Connect it to a real object from your own life
